statement: Bændasamtök Íslands (the Icelandic Farmers Association, BÍ) is officially opposed to Iceland's EU membership, a position maintained consistently since the 2009 membership application. BÍ argues that EU membership and the Common Agricultural Policy would undermine Icelandic agriculture by removing tariff protection (30–100%+ on dairy, meat, and key products), exposing small-scale Icelandic farms to competition from larger, lower-cost EU producers, and replacing Iceland's tailored subsidy system with CAP payments that may not adequately support production in harsh subarctic conditions. However, BÍ has also taken the pragmatic step of formulating minimum requirements (lágmarkskröfur) for Icelandic agriculture in any EU accession scenario, calling on the government to ensure that EU membership would not undermine the operational conditions and investments of active farmers. Internal divisions exist: farmer meetings (bændafundir) held around Iceland have revealed that not all farmers share the association leadership's certainty that opposition is the right position.
source_name: Bændasamtök Íslands — public statements and position papers (2009–2026); Vísir reporting
caveats: BÍ's position represents the institutional stance of the association's leadership, not necessarily all individual farmers. The 'minimum requirements' approach suggests tacit acknowledgment that membership remains possible and preparations should be made. BÍ is one of Iceland's most influential interest groups and has significant lobbying power, particularly through Framsóknarflokkurinn (Progressive Party), historically the farmers' party.

related_entries.statement: Icelandic agricultural subsidies totalled approximately ISK 20–22 billion (roughly €130–145 million) per year as of 2024, primarily through direct payments to sheep and dairy farmers. This equates to approximately 55–60% of gross farm revenue being derived from government support, making Iceland one of the most heavily subsidised agricultural sectors in the OECD. The OECD Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was 54% in 2023, compared to the EU average of approximately 19%.

The agriculture chapter (Chapter 11) was never opened during Iceland's EU accession negotiations (2010–2013). Iceland was invited to submit a negotiating position on agriculture but never did so, reportedly because of irreconcilable disagreements within Iceland between farming interests, the negotiating team, and political parties. The six chapters that were never opened included agriculture, fisheries, food safety, environment, regional policy, and freedom to provide services — all considered among the most sensitive. Had negotiations continued, the core issues would have been: (1) how much domestic agricultural support Iceland could maintain, (2) whether production-linked subsidies would be permitted, (3) transition periods for tariff dismantling on dairy and meat products, and (4) potential Article 142-style national aid for harsh-climate regions. The European Commission's screening report noted Iceland's high level of agricultural protection as a key challenge.

Multiple studies have assessed the potential impact of EU membership on Icelandic agriculture. The Icelandic Chamber of Commerce's 2012 assessment concluded that full CAP integration without special provisions would reduce Icelandic farm incomes by 30–50%, with sheep farming most severely affected due to loss of tariff protection and lower CAP direct payments relative to Iceland's current subsidy levels. A 2011 University of Iceland study estimated that consumer food prices could fall by 15–25% under EU membership due to tariff removal, benefiting consumers but threatening farm viability. The Farmers Association (Bændasamtök Íslands) has consistently argued that EU membership would be 'devastating' for Icelandic agriculture without permanent derogations. Pro-EU analyses counter that CAP structural funds and rural development programmes could offset income losses and modernise the sector.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Bændasamtökin sögðu í dag að núverandi stefna ESB henti ekki íslenskum landbúnaði.

citations.report_title: ESB aðild hefði veruleg neikvæð áhrif á hagsmuni íslenskra bænda

claims.claim_text: Bændasamtökin eru ekki á móti því að þjóðin fái að taka ákvörðun um hvort halda eigi áfram aðildarviðræðum við Evrópusambandið.
Bændasamtökin hafa ekki komið auga á nein sérstök tækifæri fyrir heildarhagsmuni ísleńsks landbúnaðar með ESB-aðild.
Samkeppnisstaða íslenskra bænda er skökk gagnvart stóru matvælaframleiðslulöndunum innan ESB.
Bændasamtökin munu taka virkan þátt í samtali við stjórnvöld og almenning um afleiðingar ESB-aðildar fyrir ísleńskan landbúnað ef af viðræðum verður.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Bændasamtökin hafa ekkert á móti þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um áframhaldandi viðræður.
Landbúnaður er einn sá málaflokkur sem yrði fyrir umfangsmestu og varanlegum breytingum við ESB-aðild Íslands.
Bændasamtökin gera athugasemd við upplýsingaóreiðu um ESB-aðildarmálið.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Í aðildarviðræðunum lagði Ísland fram tíu varnarlínur í landbúnaðarmálum.
Meðal varnarlína Íslands í landbúnaðarmálum voru kröfur um að tollvernd yrði haldið áfram.
Ráðherra landbúnaðarmála gerði tollverndina að forgangsmáli sínu í aðildarviðræðunum.
Ísland vildi halda sömu landbúnaðarstefnu áfram í grundvallaratriðum en það var ekki í boði þar sem sameiginleg landbúnaðarstefna ESB er grunnurinn.
Á tíma aðildarviðræðnanna var stjórnsýsla landbúnaðarmála á Íslandi að miklum hluta í höndum Bændasamtakanna, sem er ekki í samræmi við reglur ESB.
Ísland vildi fá sérreglur eða aðlögunartíma og viðhalda tollvernd og stuðningskerfum í landbúnaði.
